Hallett examines affinities between Sulpicia's poems and, among other topics, Vergil's treatment of Dido. Nadeau argues that Vergil establishes the harpy Celaeno as a trope for Sextus Pompeius. Solin shows that inscriptional testimony allows us to conclude that the river name Liris could also be used as a personal name (cf. Aen. 1 1.670). Ziolkowski explores the theme of heroic hesitation and its correlation with civic crisis through a discussion of the character of Aeneas and of a number of other heroes from antiquity to modernist times. raw_text: Aldo Setaioli. "Le Virgile d'Horace." Promethe 84. Fréchet discusses Aen. 6.635-676. Knox suggests that the first book of Tibullus may have been published in 29 B.C. rather than in the mid-20s. This opens the door to a better contextualized examination of pastoral motifs in Tibullus and his relationship to Vergil's Eclogues. Lütkemeyer discusses Ovid's intertextual allusion to the first Eclogue. Pucci suggests that "The transgression Vergil and Horace practice in 1.3 is poetic composition itself." Putnam contains a chapter on Vergil.

"Une constante de l'imaginaire virgilien: la complémentarité des contraires, comme condition de la complexité," in Luciano Cristante, ed., Incontri triestini di filologia classica. 4: 2004-2005. Polymnia: studi di filologia classica; 6. Trieste. 2006. 151-60. Booth comments on Aen. 1.263-96. Hardie comments on the "allusive plot of incestuous brother-sister marriage" which runs through Vergil's story of Dido and Aeneas, signaled both by imagery and by allusion to Callimachus. Otón Sobrino discusses the theme of existential suffering in Vergil.
